@graph.name: Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 Petrol Engine Era Map
Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 Petrol Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned V‑Class petrol engine typically costs £2,500–£8,500 supply only depending on generation and engine type. Mercedes engine replacement costs can range from $5,000 to $15,000 or more depending on complexity. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The M111 (W638 V200) is generally robust — many go on for 250,000–300,000 miles — but has common issues: oil leaks into the wiring loom, MAF sensor failure, and timing chain stretch. The M274 (W447 V200/V250) has well‑documented thermostat failures, camshaft actuator noise and timing chain rattle, carbon buildup, and fuel pump recall issues on the V250. Regular maintenance is essential.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M111 oil leaks into the wiring loom and ECU (W638 V200), M111 MAF sensor failure, M111 timing chain stretch, M274 thermostat failure (W447), M274 camshaft actuator noise and timing chain rattle (W447), M274 carbon buildup from direct injection (W447), and V250 fuel pump recall (W447 V250). See the Era Map above for generation‑specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well‑kept W447 V250 (values £15,000–£35,000+), a £4,500–£5,500 reconditioned engine is 15–30% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a solid example. Against an older W638 V200 (£2,000–£6,000), a £3,000–£4,000 recon is 40–80% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a good example. The Mercedes scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: W638 V200 (1997–2003) = M111.948 / M111.950 (2.0L, 129 bhp); W447 V200 (2014–2020) = M274 (2.0L, 163 bhp); W447 V250 (2014–present) = M274 (2.0L, 211 bhp). The W447 V250 uses the M274.920 engine. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M111 oil leak into the wiring loom, or does it require engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The oil leak into the wiring loom is repairable without replacing the engine — if caught early. The camshaft adjuster magnet seal can be replaced (£200–£400) and the wiring harness can be cleaned. However, if oil has reached the ECU, more extensive work may be required. At the first sign of oil in wiring connectors, book an inspection immediately.

eraMap.title: The V‑CLASS V200 / V250 PETROL THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Early M111 petrol — 129 bhp, 185 Nm; timing chain stretch, oil leaks into wiring loom and MAF sensor failure are the key watchpoints
M274 2.0‑litre turbo — 163 bhp, 350 Nm; thermostat failure, camshaft actuator noise and timing chain rattle are well‑documented
Higher‑output M274 — 211 bhp, 350 Nm; same M274 weaknesses with additional fuel pump recall concerns

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current V‑Class petrol range. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with fitted costs around £1,800–£2,800 for standard engines. Mercedes engine replacement costs can range from $5,000 to $15,000 or more depending on complexity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a V‑Class petrol engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The W447's transverse engine layout makes the engine bay tight but accessible. [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet]

cta.label: → Compare V‑Class petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M111 Oil Leak into Wiring Loom (W638 V200)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, O2 sensor or MAF faults, erratic engine behaviour, oil visible on wiring loom connectors, ECU failure in severe cases.
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (wiring loom cleaning or replacement, camshaft adjuster magnet seal replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if oil reaches ECU and causes failure)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you notice oil in wiring connectors, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: A well‑known issue with the M111 engine is oil leaking from the camshaft adjuster magnet (or its seal), allowing oil to ingress into the wiring harness. If left unaddressed, the oil can travel through the loom and reach the ECU, causing expensive electrical failures. Regularly check the wiring harness connectors for oil contamination. At the first sign of oil in the loom, have the camshaft adjuster seal replaced and the wiring cleaned or replaced.

problems.title: M111 MAF Sensor Failure (W638 V200)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, misfires, rough idle, jerky acceleration, poor fuel economy, bad gear changes (automatic).

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect drivability and fuel economy

problems.recommendation: The M111's Mass Air Flow (MAF) sensor has a modest lifespan. When it fails, the engine becomes "a horrid drive, misfires and loss of power and bad gear changes". Symptoms include sluggish acceleration and poor fuel economy. If you experience any of these, have the MAF sensor inspected and replaced if necessary. Use only genuine or high‑quality OEM sensors.

problems.title: M111 Timing Chain Stretch (W638 V200)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, metallic clattering, engine warning light, loss of power, rough running.
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£5,000 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Timing chain stretch is a known issue on higher‑mileage M111 engines. A cold‑start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston‑valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct Mercedes‑approved oil are the best prevention. At the first sign of rattle, have the timing system inspected immediately.

problems.title: M274 Thermostat Failure (W447 V200 / V250)
problems.symptoms: Engine taking too long to reach operating temperature, temperature gauge fluctuations, reduced fuel economy, check engine light.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless overheating leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if temperature fluctuations occur

problems.recommendation: The most common problem with the M274 engine is a failed thermostat, which is costly to repair compared to thermostat replacement costs on other engines. If you notice the engine taking longer than usual to warm up or temperature fluctuations, have the thermostat inspected. Proactive replacement is recommended at 60,000‑mile intervals.

problems.title: M274 Camshaft Actuator Noise & Timing Chain Rattle (W447 V200 / V250)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or ticking noise from the engine on cold start lasting 2‑3 seconds, metallic noises from the timing area, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M274 engine suffers from camshaft actuator (solenoid) noise, usually appearing after 60,000–70,000 miles. Timing chain rattle on cold start is also a known concern. One of the most common problems is a metallic crackling or rattling noise for 2‑3 seconds on cold start, caused by wear of the camshaft phasers. If you hear any unusual noises from the timing area, have the engine inspected immediately.

problems.title: M274 Direct Injection Carbon Buildup (W447 V200 / V250)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced fuel economy, misfires.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; can affect performance and fuel economy

problems.recommendation: Like all direct‑injection petrol engines, the M274 suffers from carbon buildup on the intake valves. This can cause loss of power, rough idle, and hesitation. Regular use of premium fuel and occasional Italian tune‑ups can help reduce buildup. If symptoms persist, have the intake valves cleaned professionally (walnut blasting).

problems.title: V250 Fuel Pump Recall (W447 V250)
problems.symptoms: Sudden loss of power, engine stalling, inability to restart, fuel system warnings.
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£8,500 (if fuel system failure leads to engine damage)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: check recall status immediately; can cause sudden power loss

problems.recommendation: Mercedes‑Benz has issued recalls for the V250 (and Vito Tourer) due to a fuel pump issue that could cause the vehicle to suddenly lose power. The fuel pump assembly may not meet specifications, posing a risk of collision or accident. Check your vehicle's recall status immediately — if your V250 hasn't had this work completed, get it done. This is a safety‑critical item.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes V‑Class V200 / V250 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: M111 oil leak into wiring loom
rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it before ECU damage occurs
Catch it early — far cheaper than engine replacement
Proactive replacement prevents fuel economy loss

rows.problem: M274 camshaft actuator / timing chain rattle

rows.whenItMakesSense: Both M111 and M274 are robust; catastrophic failures are rare

buyingChecks.title: Buying This V‑Class Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using Mercedes‑approved oil
* M111 (W638): wiring loom checked for oil contamination; MAF sensor replaced recently
* M111 (W638): no cold‑start rattle — timing chain should be healthy
* M274 (W447): thermostat replaced or functioning correctly
* M274 (W447): no cold‑start rattle or ticking from the timing area
* V250: fuel pump recall completed (check with Mercedes)
avoidIf: * No proof of regular maintenance — these engines are durable but unforgiving of neglect
* M111 with oil in wiring connectors — ECU damage risk is high
* M111 with cold‑start rattle — timing chain work is imminent
* M274 with cold‑start rattle or ticking — camshaft actuator or timing chain issues
* V250 with no proof of fuel pump recall — safety‑critical item
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — these engines can hide expensive faults
* "Cheap" examples — the purchase price is the least of your worries

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Mercedes V‑Class V200 and V250 petrol represent two distinct eras of Mercedes MPV engineering. The W638 V200 (1997–2003) is a robust workhorse with the M111 engine — capable of 250,000–300,000 miles with proper maintenance — but oil leaks into the wiring loom, MAF sensor failures, and timing chain stretch are the key watchpoints. The W447 V200/V250 (2014–present) uses the M274 2.0‑litre turbo — a modern engine with strong performance but well‑documented thermostat failures, camshaft actuator noise and timing chain rattle, carbon buildup from direct injection, and fuel pump recall issues on the V250. Buy on service history, prioritise recall work completion, and maintain on a regular schedule. The V‑Class is a reliable and practical vehicle overall — but like all Mercedes, it demands careful ownership.

cta.label: → Compare V‑Class petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes V‑Class V200 or V250 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Wiring Loom (M111) — Check for oil contamination in wiring connectors; oil can travel to the ECU
* Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the engine (M111 timing chain stretch; M274 camshaft actuator / timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M111/M274) and confirmed oil‑change discipline
* Thermostat (M274) — Does the engine warm up quickly? Thermostat failure is common
* MAF Sensor (M111) — Any loss of power or rough idle? (MAF sensor failure warning)
* Fuel Pump Recall (V250) — Has the recall been completed? (safety‑critical)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, MAF, thermostat, or fuel system codes are most common

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows exactly match Section 1B's Era Map — the same engine codes, years, and generation coverage.
